plainMeaning: Having confessed the twelve vows, the reciter now admits every way they have been careless with sacred learning, with reverence for the Jina and guru, and with their fasts and penances.
recitation: 1. tathā jñānācāre jñāna rahe āśātanā kīdhī hoya. paḍhatāṁ, guṇatāṁ antarāya kīdho hoya. macchara dharyo hoya. akṣarane thūṁka lagāḍyuṁ hoya. pāṭī, pothī, kavalī, ṭhavaṇī, nokāravālī, kāgale paga lagāḍyo hoya. khamāsamaṇa pākhe parivāḍī līdhī hoya. jñānadravyataṇī sārasambhāḷa kīdhī na hoya. aneruṁ e jñānācāra viṣe. ॥ 1 ॥ 2. tathā darśanācāre samyaktva virādhiuṁ hoya. deva, guru namaskāra karyā pākhe bhojana kīdhuṁ hoya. deva pūjatāṁ dhotī śuddha kīdhī na hoya. mukhakośa kīdho na hoya. ādhaḍaviche deva pūjyā hoya. gurune āsaṇe, besaṇe, upagaraṇe paga lagāḍyo hoya, guruvacana dveṣalage vālyuṁ hoya. bimba hāthathakī vichuṭyuṁ hoya, bimbanuṁ vecāvacca kīdhuṁ na hoya, devadravya āpaṇe vyavasāye ghālyuṁ hoya. devadravya viṇasatuṁ uvekhyuṁ hoya. aneruṁ e darśanācāra viṣe. ॥ 2 ॥ 3. tapācāre chatī śakte paccakkhāṇa kīdhuṁ na hoya, karīne bhāṅgyuṁ hoya, paccakkhāṇa pālyuṁ na hoya, niyama, abhigraha leï bhāṅgyā hoya, cha bheda abhyantara, cha bheda bāhya tapa avasare sācavyuṁ na hoya. aneruṁ e tapācāra viṣe. ॥ 3 ॥

wordByWord.gloss: and likewise; moreover (linking this section to the foregoing confession)
wordByWord.notes: Opens both the jñānācāra (1) and darśanācāra (2) sections; signals a new heading within the same confession.

wordByWord.gloss: in (the matter of) the conduct concerning knowledge
wordByWord.notes: First of the five ācāras; here the heading under which faults against learning are confessed.

wordByWord.gloss: towards, in respect of (idiomatic: 'as regards knowledge')
wordByWord.etymology: Old Guj. rahe 'remaining, with regard to'
wordByWord.notes: Functions here as a postpositional connective rather than the literal verb 'remains'.
Key technical term: any act demeaning what is holy — here, scripture and learning.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Guj. kīdhuṁ, past part. of karavuṁ 'to do' (Skt. kṛ-)
wordByWord.notes: Agrees in gender with the fault-noun; recurs as kīdho (m.) / kīdhuṁ (n.) below.

wordByWord.gloss: may have been (auxiliary, hypothetical 'should it be the case')
wordByWord.notes: The recurring subjunctive auxiliary closing every clause: 'if [such a fault] has occurred'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. guṇ- 'to repeat, count over'; gerund -tāṁ

wordByWord.notes: Doctrinally also the name of a karma type; here the everyday act of obstructing learning.
Wetting the finger to turn manuscript pages defiles the written word — a classic jñāna-āśātanā.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Guj. lagāḍavuṁ 'to apply, attach' (Skt. lag-)

wordByWord.gloss: the cloth wrapper / cover for a scripture-book
wordByWord.notes: One of the knowledge-implements deserving reverence; exact form per book orthography.

wordByWord.gloss: (may have) let touch — i.e. touched with the foot — masculine
wordByWord.notes: Touching scripture or its implements with the foot is gravely disrespectful.

wordByWord.gloss: the khamāsamaṇa prostration (kṣamā-śramaṇa salutation to the guru)

wordByWord.notes: Recurs in section 2 ('namaskāra karyā pākhe').

wordByWord.gloss: the ordered round / sequence of recitation (of sūtras for study)
wordByWord.notes: Taking up the study-round without first making the khamāsamaṇa is the fault.

wordByWord.gloss: of the knowledge-funds (resources endowed for learning/scriptures)
wordByWord.etymology: jñāna + dravya 'fund, property' + Old Guj. genitive taṇī 'of'

wordByWord.notes: Marks the faults of omission: 'has not [done]'.
The sweep-up word: 'whatever else'; closes each section.
'…regarding this jñānācāra/darśanācāra/tapācāra'.

wordByWord.gloss: section-marker one (close of the jñānācāra portion)
wordByWord.notes: Local sub-numbering 1–3 within this verse; not independent shlokas.

wordByWord.gloss: in the conduct concerning right faith

wordByWord.notes: Defined in the preface; the object that may have been violated.
In Jain usage the vītarāga Jina, not a creator-god.
Eating before paying homage to deva and guru is the lapse.

wordByWord.gloss: the dhotī (loincloth worn for temple worship)
the mouth-cloth (tied over mouth/nose during pūjā)
wordByWord.notes: Worn so the breath does not defile the worship.

wordByWord.gloss: carelessly, in a slipshod / half-attentive manner
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; glossed from context as worship done without due care (see notes).
Touching the guru's seat or gear with the foot is the disrespect confessed.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. dveṣa 'aversion' + Old Guj. lage 'on account of'

wordByWord.notes: Deflecting or contradicting the guru's word out of spite.
Letting the sacred image fall from one's grip while handling it.
Failing to render due care/maintenance to the image is the omission.

wordByWord.gloss: consecrated temple/deity funds (property dedicated to the Jina)
(may have) put in, diverted, used up
wordByWord.notes: Diverting consecrated funds into personal commerce — a serious misappropriation.

wordByWord.gloss: (may have) ignored, looked on indifferently, neglected
wordByWord.notes: Standing by while temple funds are wasted is itself a fault.

wordByWord.gloss: section-marker two (close of the darśanācāra portion)
in (one's) strength/capacity — 'chatī śakte' = though having the ability
pratyākhyāna — a renunciation/abstinence vow (e.g. a fast)
wordByWord.notes: Book spelling with doubled conjunct preserved verbatim.
The six inner tapas: prāyaścitta, vinaya, vaiyāvṛtya, svādhyāya, vyutsarga (kāyotsarga), dhyāna.
The six outer tapas: anaśana, ūnodarī, vṛtti-saṅkṣepa, rasa-parityāga, kāya-kleśa, saṁlīnatā.

wordByWord.gloss: at the proper occasion / when the time came
section-marker three (close of the tapācāra portion)

literalTranslation: And, regarding the conduct of knowledge: irreverence may have been shown toward knowledge; obstruction may have been caused to one reading or reciting; envy may have been harbored; spittle may have been applied to the letters; the foot may have touched the writing-board, the book, the book-cloth, the book-rest, the rosary, or paper; the study-round may have been taken up without the khamāsamaṇa; the knowledge-funds may not have been cared for — and whatever else regarding this jñānācāra. ॥1॥ And, regarding the conduct of faith: right faith may have been violated; a meal may have been taken without saluting deva and guru; while worshipping the Jina the dhotī may not have been kept pure; the mouth-cloth may not have been worn; the Jina may have been worshipped carelessly; the foot may have touched the guru's seat, cushion, or implements; the guru's word may have been rebuffed out of malice; the image may have slipped from the hand; service to the image may not have been rendered; deity-funds may have been put into one's own business; deity-funds going to waste may have been ignored — and whatever else regarding this darśanācāra. ॥2॥ Regarding the conduct of austerity: though able, a pratyākhyāna may not have been made; having made it, it may have been broken; a pratyākhyāna may not have been kept; rules and resolves taken up may have been broken; the six kinds of inner and six kinds of outer austerity may not have been observed at the proper time — and whatever else regarding this tapācāra. ॥3॥
idiomaticTranslation: Now I turn to my conduct in the three remaining domains of right practice. In knowledge: have I slighted what is sacred — broken another's study, nursed envy of a learned one, wet my finger to the holy letters, let my foot fall on tablet, manuscript, book-cloth, stand, rosary or paper, begun my recitation without first bowing in khamāsamaṇa, or neglected the funds set aside for learning? In faith: have I wounded my own right conviction — eaten before bowing to the Jina and guru, worshipped in an unclean dhotī or without the mouth-cloth, done the rite carelessly, set my foot to the guru's seat or gear, spurned his word in spite, let the sacred image slip from my hands, failed in its upkeep, slipped temple wealth into my own trade, or looked away while it was squandered? In austerity: when I had the strength, did I leave a fast untaken, take it and break it, fail to carry one through, breach the rules and resolves I had vowed, or let slip the six inner and six outer penances when their hour came? For each of these, and whatever else in these three, I confess.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the grand tally of eighty-five vow-transgressions sealed in the preceding passage, the confession pivots from *what* is vowed to *how* it is lived: the pañcācāra, the five-fold conduct. This verse takes three of the five — jñāna (knowledge), darśana (faith) and tapa (austerity) — leaving vīrya for the next verse. Strikingly, cāritrācāra (conduct proper) is *not* enumerated separately here, because its substance has just been exhausted in the twelve-vow confession itself; the reciter should not look for a 'missing' fourth heading. What gives this verse its texture is its descent from the abstract to the palpably physical. Knowledge is not defended as a doctrine but as a set of objects one's body must honor: the pāṭī, pothī, kavalī, ṭhavaṇī, nokāravālī and bare paper that a careless foot or a moistened finger can defile. Faith, likewise, is audited through the hands and feet — an unclean dhotī, an absent mukhakośa, an image let slip, temple funds quietly folded into one's ledger. Only in the tapa section does the verse turn inward, and there it leans on a fixed enumeration the advanced reciter is expected to unfold: *cha bheda abhyantara, cha bheda bāhya* — the six inner and six outer austerities. The refrain *aneruṁ … e … viṣe* ('whatever else regarding this') closes each section in the same breath, gathering the unnamed with the named. The genius of the passage is its insistence that reverence is enacted, not merely felt: a faith that lets the Jina's image fall, or a learning that rests a foot on scripture, has already lapsed before any belief is questioned.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the classical Jain teaching that right practice has five channels — jñānācāra, darśanācāra, cāritrācāra, tapācāra and vīryācāra — each with its own canonical sub-transgressions. By confessing jñāna, darśana and tapa here (and vīrya next), the reciter completes a second axis of self-audit orthogonal to the twelve vows: the vows ask *which restraints did I breach?*, the ācāras ask *with what quality did I know, believe, and discipline myself?* The tapa section is doctrinally precise: the 'six and six' are the standard external austerities (anaśana, ūnodarī, vṛtti-saṅkṣepa, rasa-parityāga, kāya-kleśa, saṁlīnatā) and internal austerities (prāyaścitta, vinaya, vaiyāvṛtya, svādhyāya, vyutsarga, dhyāna) of the Tattvārtha tradition — twelvefold tapas as the active burner of karma. The darśana faults bind right faith to concrete reverence (deva, guru, bimba, devadravya), reflecting that for a layperson samyaktva is sustained or strained through devotional conduct, not abstract assent alone. Misappropriating devadravya is singled out because consecrated wealth carries a heavier karmic charge than ordinary property.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The verse converts to a vivid examination of conscience the modern reciter can use directly: Did I handle scriptures and prayer-beads with clean, careful hands — never with a foot, never with a licked finger? Did I bow before I began study? Did I keep my fasts and resolves when I had the strength, or abandon them for convenience? Did I treat temple property as sacred and never as my own? Its lesson is that devotion lives in small, bodily habits — and that neglect (the 'na hoya' faults of *not* caring, *not* keeping, *not* serving) weighs as much as active wrongdoing.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra ch. 9 (the twelvefold tapas: six bāhya + six abhyantara)
crossReferences.source: Umāsvāti, ed./tr. various (e.g. Vijay K. Jain)

crossReferences.text: The five ācāras (jñāna, darśana, cāritra, tapa, vīrya) of the Āvaśyaka tradition
Laghu Atiyāra — the short Devasi/Rāi confession that this extended form replaces
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.)

sources.title: Pratikramana
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the six-āvaśyaka frame and that the fourth āvaśyaka is the confession/repentance unit into which this atichāra recitation falls.

sources.title: Tattvārtha Sūtra (Umāsvāti)
sources.consultedFor: The classical six external + six internal austerities (cha bheda bāhya / abhyantara tapa) and the doctrinal status of tapas as karma-shedding.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: The verbatim Gujarati confession text, its orthography (paccakkhāṇa, atiyāra), and the placement of the pañcācāra confession after the eighty-five-atichāra tally.

sources.title: Anuvrata (the twelve lay vows)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming why cāritrācāra is not separately confessed here — its content is the just-completed twelve-vow audit.

notes: Verse-specific caveats: (1) 'ādhaḍaviche' (આધડવિચે) is a rare Deśya adverb; rendered from context as 'carelessly / half-attentively', but the exact form is uncertain and flagged. (2) 'kavalī' and 'ṭhavaṇī' are study-/worship-implements; 'kavalī' is read as the scripture book-cloth and 'ṭhavaṇī' as the folding book-rest (also the sthāpanā-stand) — local usage may vary. (3) 'vecāvacca' (વેચાવચ્ચ) = Prakrit veyāvacca / Skt vaiyāvṛtya, 'service/upkeep', not commerce. (4) The local section numbers 1–3 are sub-headings within this single verse (jñāna / darśana / tapa), not independent shlokas; cāritrācāra is intentionally absent because it coincides with the preceding twelve-vow confession, and vīryācāra follows in the next verse. (5) Book orthography preserved verbatim, including the doubled conjunct in 'paccakkhāṇa' and 'atiyāra' (with ય). (6) The auxiliary 'hoya' and the participles 'kīdhī/kīdho/kīdhuṁ' recur throughout; glossed once and not repeated per occurrence.
